params.text:
Gaining consent

Consent means the person agrees to the care or support you are about to give, having understood what it involves. You must gain consent every time — it is not a one-off signature but an ongoing part of care.

  • Explain clearly what you are going to do and why, in a way the person understands.
  • Give them time to ask questions and to say no.
  • Consent can be given in words, in writing, or non-verbally (for example, a nod or holding out an arm).
  • A person can withdraw consent at any point, and you must respect that.

Under the Mental Capacity Act 2005, always assume a person has capacity to decide unless it is shown otherwise, and take all practical steps to help them make their own decision. If someone genuinely lacks capacity for a particular decision, follow the Act and involve the right people to act in their best interests.

params.taskDescription: Drag the words into the gaps to complete these key points about consent.
params.textField: You must gain *consent* before providing care, and it can be *withdrawn* at any time. Under the Mental Capacity Act you should always *assume* a person has capacity unless proven otherwise. Consent can be given in words, in writing or *non-verbally*. Always give the person time to ask *questions* and to say *no*.
